title: Building Inspection Services

evidence_facts.title: Construction and Building Inspectors : Occupational Outlook Handbook
evidence_facts.quote: Home inspectors typically examine houses, condominiums, townhomes, and other dwellings to report on their structure and overall condition.

scope.population: Construction and building inspectors; home inspectors are one described specialty
scope.industry_mismatch: Occupation source includes inspectors outside private NAICS 541350, including public-sector and construction inspection work.

evidence_facts.title: Construction and Building Inspectors : Occupational Outlook Handbook
evidence_facts.quote: Although construction and building inspectors spend most of their time examining worksites, they also spend time in an office reviewing blueprints, writing reports, and scheduling inspections.

scope.industry_mismatch: Occupation source is broader than private building-inspection establishments in NAICS 541350.

evidence_facts.title: Palmtech | Home Inspection Software + Report Writer
evidence_facts.quote: Schedule inspections with a few quick taps and write reports using AI-smart tools for photo review and documentation – built to keep inspections moving.

scope.population: New and seasoned home inspectors using or considering Palmtech software
scope.industry_mismatch: Vendor product claim; it does not measure adoption by U.S. NAICS 541350 firms.

evidence_facts.title: Frequently Asked Questions | WIN Home Inspection Franchise
evidence_facts.quote: As of the end of 2024, WIN currently has 279 franchise owners. We’re the fastest-growing franchise in the industry!

scope.industry_mismatch: One franchisor's network; not a count of NAICS 541350 firms, acquirers, or transactions.

evidence_facts.title: HouseMaster Home Inspections
evidence_facts.quote: In 1979 he created HouseMaster, the first home inspection franchise operation in the US, which remains the first and oldest home inspection franchise system in North America.

scope.industry_mismatch: One branded franchise system, not a NAICS-wide consolidator or merger count.

evidence_facts.title: Comprehensive Inspections | AmeriSpec®
evidence_facts.quote: AmeriSpec inspections are conducted by individually owned and operated franchise business owners who live and work in your local community.

scope.population: AmeriSpec inspectors and local franchise business owners
scope.industry_mismatch: One branded franchise network, not a NAICS-wide consolidation or acquisition measure.

evidence_facts.title: Buyer, Seller Agreements to Waive Inspections Catch the Attention of State Governments
evidence_facts.quote: NAR members reported 21% of buyers had waived the inspection contingency, up from 18% one month earlier and up from 17% in September 2024.

scope.population: Buyers reported by National Association of REALTORS® members
scope.unit: percent of buyers waiving an inspection contingency
scope.industry_mismatch: Residential real-estate transaction behavior, not a measure of NAICS 541350 firms, prices, or labor.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541350 ($1,864,147,476) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541350 ($3,995,784,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $146,226 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541350); class <5: 6,523 firms, avg revenue/firm $193,991 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $30,360 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 612 firms, avg revenue/firm $827,992 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $129,581 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 305 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,798,961 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $281,537 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 143 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,816,294 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $910,250 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $18,547,304 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,902,653 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,508,765 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,835,622 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 40
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

scope_mismatches: F1 and F2 describe an occupation broader than private NAICS 541350 and do not report task-time shares.
F3 is a vendor feature description and does not measure adoption, time saved, or role composition for NAICS 541350 firms.
The deterministic wage weights are from parent 4-digit 5413 and are not a validated six-digit workforce mix.
bridge_steps: Use F1 and F2 only to distinguish physical examination and testing from office report and scheduling work.
Use F3 only to establish that AI-oriented photo-review and report-writing tooling is offered to inspectors.
Map those task categories to each required supplied SOC without changing its supplied wage share.
Apply a separate residual midpoint because the supplied roles do not identify the remaining wage share.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select within-role fractions that credit AI with documentation and coordination work but retain human physical inspection, technical judgment, customer accountability, and field activity.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: The supplied civil-engineer role is a parent-code proxy. Documentation and plan review can be AI-assisted, while site, test, and signoff work remains human-led.
The supplied architect role has document and plan work exposed to AI assistance, but client coordination and site judgment remain.
Project-management administration, scheduling, and status documentation are relatively automatable; relationship and accountability work remain.
Drafting and report-production tasks are document-intensive, although field verification and technical review remain human work.
Scheduling and routine administrative decisions can automate, while local referral relationships, staffing, and accountability remain human-led.
The supplied mechanical-engineer role has analysis and documentation exposure but retains physical systems judgment and field verification.
Management reporting and workflow can automate, but technical supervision, customer accountability, and hiring remain human work.
The physical walkthrough, testing, and condition judgment persist, while report drafting, photo review, and scheduling can be AI-assisted.
The supplied electrical-engineer role has analysis and report work exposed to AI assistance, while inspection and technical responsibility remain.
Software-development work has material coding assistance exposure, but the role is a parent-code artifact rather than a demonstrated building-inspection role.
The large unlisted wage remainder is not identified in the supplied parent-code mix, so its automation fraction is a deliberately broad midpoint estimate.

pi_dist.rationale: A one-off inspection serves a buyer decision and includes material physical work, supporting some operator retention of report-efficiency savings; inspection-contingency waivers show that transactional demand and referral competition constrain that retention.

scope_mismatches: F1 describes home-inspector duties, not prices, payer contracts, or cost pass-through.
F7 measures buyers waiving contingencies, not the price response to AI or the distribution of savings among firms.
bridge_steps: Use F1 to identify an in-person inspection product supplied to buyers or sellers in a transaction.
Use F7 to recognize that a meaningful share of buyers can forgo the contingency, creating demand-side pressure.
Treat report efficiency as an operator cost reduction rather than evidence of a price change.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Choose 0.65 as a moderate-to-high operator-retention estimate, below full retention because transaction competition and waived inspections can pressure price and volume.

pi_moat.rationale: Physical condition assessment and the buyer-decision context support a service moat, but the documented waiver rate shows that paid inspection demand is not universal.

scope_mismatches: F1 does not measure competitive differentiation, transferability of referral relationships, or customer switching.
F7 does not measure firm moats and covers only reported contingency waivers.
bridge_steps: Use F1 to identify a physical assessment and written condition report as the core service.
Use F7 to identify a demand-side option to waive an inspection contingency.
Do not treat either fact as evidence of a firm-specific moat.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Choose a midrange moat estimate because the service involves physical inspection and trust, while a portion of transactions can dispense with the contingency and local referral relationships may not transfer cleanly.

t50_years.rationale: AI-oriented report and photo-review tooling is available, but the core work retains onsite examination and there is no measured NAICS 541350 firm-adoption series in the located evidence.

scope_mismatches: F1 and F2 are occupational descriptions rather than adoption measurements or forecasts.
F3 documents a vendor feature, not the share of U.S. NAICS 541350 firms using AI materially.
No located source forecasts the date on which half of target firms will materially use AI in automatable functions.
bridge_steps: Use F3 to establish availability of AI-oriented report and photo-review tooling.
Use F1 and F2 to separate automatable office work from onsite examination.
Treat the current adoption estimate as below 50 percent because no target-population measurement was located.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Forecast five years from the run date to 50 percent material firm adoption, recognizing that this is an unobserved diffusion judgment rather than a source value.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: A home-inspection software vendor advertises AI-oriented report and photo-review tools, but no source located in this run measures the share of U.S. NAICS 541350 firms materially using them.

scope_mismatches: F3 is a vendor feature description with no U.S. firm count, customer count, adoption rate, period, or NAICS 541350 population measure.
bridge_steps: Use F3 only as evidence that relevant AI-oriented tools are commercially available to inspectors.
Do not convert software availability into an observed adoption rate.
Set the target population as U.S. firms in NAICS 541350 materially using AI in automatable functions.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 10 percent as a low early-adoption estimate for the target population; it is not a vendor-user or worker-use statistic.

historical_analogs.value: Report-writing and scheduling digitization are the relevant analog: office documentation can standardize and accelerate while the onsite condition assessment remains a separately performed physical service.
historical_analogs.rationale: The located evidence supports an onsite-plus-office workflow and the availability of AI-oriented office tooling, not a measured historical adoption curve or financial outcome.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Incremental workflow augmentation to substantial report-process substitution

scope_mismatches: F1 and F2 are occupational descriptions, not historical technology case studies.
F3 is a current vendor feature description, not historical evidence on prices, labor hours, or firm outcomes.
bridge_steps: Use F1 and F2 to identify the split between physical site work and office reporting or scheduling.
Use F3 to identify current AI-oriented assistance for the office workflow.
Describe the analog without asserting a measured historical causal result.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Characterize the analogy as workflow augmentation with partial process substitution, not as an observed financial precedent.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: No directly applicable, live, firm-owner age distribution for U.S. NAICS 541350 was located in the assigned research scope.

scope_mismatches: none; no source fact is used because no target-population owner-age statistic was located
bridge_steps: Searched public home-inspector association and industry materials for owner-age or 60-plus distributions.
Located no source in this run that measures owners of U.S. NAICS 541350 firms on the requested threshold.
Avoid treating worker, association-member, or broader small-business demographics as the target owner population.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Use 30 percent only as a broad planning estimate for a small-business owner population, not as an observed owner-age share.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No directly verified source located in this run documents a succession shortage for owners of U.S. NAICS 541350 firms.

scope_mismatches: none; no source fact is used because this is an absence-of-located-documentation judgment
bridge_steps: Searched public home-inspector association and industry materials for succession, retirement, owner-age, and shortage evidence.
Located no direct statement documenting a NAICS 541350 owner succession shortage.
Do not infer a shortage from generic retirement openings or franchise marketing.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Set false as an absence-of-evidence estimate, not as proof that no shortage exists.

active_consolidators.rationale: Three independently evidenced national-brand franchise systems are counted as visible organized competitors or consolidator-like platforms; this is not a full M&A count.

scope_mismatches: F4 is one franchisor's owner count rather than an acquirer count.
F5 is a historical claim about one franchise system and does not enumerate current competitors.
F6 describes one franchise operating model and does not identify transactions, ownership concentration, or a full market census.
bridge_steps: Treat WIN, HouseMaster, and AmeriSpec as three separately evidenced organized franchise systems in the inspected service category.
Use the count only as a conservative proxy for visible platform competition.
Exclude local independents, unverified brands, and any claim that these are all market consolidators.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select three visible consolidator-like systems rather than claim an observed total of all acquirers or franchise networks.

buy_mult.rationale: No live comparable set located in this run states an EV/EBITDA purchase multiple after market-rate owner compensation for an acquirable NAICS 541350 target.

scope_mismatches: none; no source fact is used because available public franchise descriptions do not provide normalized target EV/EBITDA transaction multiples
bridge_steps: Searched public franchise and small-business materials for completed building-inspection transactions with EV/EBITDA after market-rate owner compensation.
Located no suitable target-size comparable in this run.
Do not map SDE, franchise investment cost, owner income, or revenue figures to EV/EBITDA without an explicit normalization.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Use 3.0x only as a low-confidence underwriting placeholder for a transferable multi-inspector target after hypothetical owner compensation normalization.

exit_mult.rationale: No live comparable set located in this run supports an AI-era exit EV/EBITDA multiple after market-rate owner compensation, so the modest step-up is purely a scenario assumption.

scope_mismatches: none; no source fact is used because no normalized exit comparables or forward transaction terms were located
bridge_steps: Retain the same target definition as the buy multiple: a transferable multi-inspector business with owner compensation normalized to market rate.
Avoid using franchise investment costs, unnormalized SDE listings, or vendor claims as transaction-multiple evidence.
Treat any AI benefit as uncertain workflow margin improvement rather than an observed valuation rerating.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Use 3.3x as a small scenario premium over the 3.0x buy assumption, contingent on retained demand and transferable operating processes.

pricing_structure.value: Predominantly per-inspection transaction fee in the residential pre-purchase segment; buyer payment and the degree of hours-based repricing are unverified assumptions in this run.
pricing_structure.rationale: The occupational source describes home inspections in the context of buyers or sellers seeking an assessment before a transaction, but it does not state the payer, fee basis, or price level.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Flat per-inspection fee with partial to material local price competition

scope_mismatches: F1 identifies a buyer-or-seller transaction context but does not report a contract payer, pricing mechanism, fee, or NAICS 541350 revenue mix.
bridge_steps: Use F1 only to locate the residential inspection service within a buyer-or-seller real-estate transaction.
Do not claim F1 proves buyer payment or a flat fee.
Apply the pricing description only to the selected dominant residential segment.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select a per-inspection transaction-fee description as a low-confidence operating assumption and explicitly leave payer and pass-through unverified.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Python supplies uplift_pp from the role-weighted labor exposure. The five-year adoption estimate is deliberately slower than immediate full diffusion because the cited workflow still includes onsite examination, while report and scheduling work can automate.

terminal_value.justification: AI can reduce report, photo-review, and scheduling effort while the onsite condition assessment remains part of the service. However, the documented inspection-contingency waiver rate shows paid demand is not universal, so mature economics depend on transaction behavior and referral relationships rather than capability alone.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V and A use only task and AI-workflow evidence plus explicitly labeled adoption and timing judgments. Franchise-system evidence informs active-consolidator context only; it is not used as a V or A proxy.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: The estimated transaction-fee structure maps potential retained report-efficiency savings into pi_dist, while waivable demand and local relationship competition limit pi_moat. No inspection-fee reduction is assumed as an observed fact.

dataset_mismatch.description: The supplied role mix is 4-digit 5413 and contains engineering, architecture, management, and software roles that are not demonstrated as the six-digit Building Inspection Services workforce. The injected labor share may also underrepresent owner-operated or contractor labor. Required SOCs and wage shares are used unchanged; the role assessment is therefore low confidence.

sensitivity.alternative_value: Use a field-inspector-heavy six-digit mix if a validated source becomes available
sensitivity.impact: The final role-weighted exposure could move materially because the parent-code technical roles and large residual do not identify the target workforce.

sensitivity.alternative_value: Higher economic labor intensity if owner and contractor compensation is material
sensitivity.impact: V and final uplift_pp would rise or fall with the deterministic labor-share limitation; no adjustment is made here.

subsegments.name: Residential pre-purchase home inspection
subsegments.evidence_or_uncertainty: Selected as dominant because the NAICS description and occupational evidence center on home condition reports for buyers or sellers; no public revenue split was located in this run.

subsegments.name: Commercial property-condition assessment and building inspection
subsegments.evidence_or_uncertainty: Included because building inspection services extend beyond dwellings, but this run located no NAICS revenue share or comparable economics for the segment.

subsegments.name: Specialty add-on inspections and testing
subsegments.evidence_or_uncertainty: Included because franchise sources list services such as radon, water, septic, and energy assessments, but no NAICS revenue share was located.

market_boundary.selection_basis: Score the residential segment because the located occupational evidence explicitly describes home inspectors reporting on dwellings for buyers or sellers; ranking remains a low-confidence market-boundary judgment.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A changes directly with the adoption-timing estimate.
Determines the base from which the t50 timing judgment is made and could require a different timing case if measured adoption is much higher.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: Field-inspector-heavy six-digit mix unavailable; within-role ranges are 0.10-0.70
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: V and final uplift_pp are highly sensitive to the parent-code role-mix mismatch and residual allocation.
M depends on the unobserved difference between normalized entry and exit multiples.

reviewer_note: All selected values are estimates because the live sources establish task context, product availability, transaction-waiver behavior, or individual franchise systems rather than the target NAICS firm-level measures. No market-rate owner-compensated transaction comparables or target owner-age statistic were located in this run.

V.arithmetic: Python finalizer v3.1.1: V_raw = labor_share × ai_replaceable_share = 0.4974 × 0.359745 = 0.178937163; V = 10 × min(1, V_raw / 0.25) = 7.15748652

C.arithmetic: Python finalizer v3.1.1: C = 10 × pi_dist × pi_moat = 10 × 0.65 × 0.55 = 3.575

A.arithmetic: Python finalizer v3.1.1: A = 10 when t50_years = 0, otherwise min(10, 10 / t50_years); t50_years = 5, A = 2

M.arithmetic: Python finalizer v3.1.1: M = clamp(4 × (exit_mult − buy_mult) / buy_mult, 0, 10) = 0.4
